data.title: Kinder Morgan tops Q1 estimates as gas demand surge offsets lower fuel volumes
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) reported first-quarter earnings above Wall Street expectations on Wednesday, helped by stronger natural gas demand tied to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and rising power needs from data centers.

Shares rose 1% in extended trading; they've risen about 20% in the 12 months through today's close.

Pipeline operators in the United States have benefited from steady oil and gas production in the Permian Basin, while uncertainty surrounding shipping routes through the Strait of Hormuz has increased demand for U.S.-sourced liquefied natural gas.

“The geopolitical landscape became even more turbulent this quarter, with conflict in the Middle East joining the ongoing war in Ukraine as a source of significant commodity price volatility,” Executive Chairman Richard Kinder said in a statement.

The company said disruptions affecting competing LNG suppliers could also improve the longer-term outlook for U.S. gas consumption. Kinder Morgan (KMI) projects total U.S. natural gas demand will reach 150 billion cubic feet per day by 2031, up roughly 27% from current levels.

Kinder Morgan (KMI), one of North America’s largest energy infrastructure companies, also said it has agreed to buy Monument Pipeline, a 225-mile natural gas pipeline network serving Houston and surrounding markets, for $505 million in cash. The deal is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026.

Looking ahead, the company forecast 2026 net income attributable to Kinder Morgan to remain steady at $3.1 billion, while adjusted earnings per share are expected to increase 5% to $1.36. It also reiterated expectations for adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $8.6 billion this year.

For the quarter ended March 31, Kinder Morgan (KMI) posted adjusted earnings of $0.48 a share, topping analysts’ consensus estimate of $0.39.

Revenue rose to $4.83 billion, ahead of expectations for $4.55 billion.

Kinder Morgan (KMI) said it moved about 49,475 billion British thermal units of natural gas a day during the quarter, up from 45,978 billion Btu a day a year earlier.

Total products volumes, which include refined fuels such as jet fuel and diesel, declined to 1,965 thousand barrels a day from 2,047 thousand barrels a day in the prior-year period.

data.title: Everforth transition continues as management forecasts Q2 revenue of $970M-$1B and adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.8%-9.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ASGN Incorporated (ASGN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “This will be our final earnings call under the ASGN name. And on Friday, we will officially begin operating as Everforth and trading under our new stock ticker, EFOR,” said (CEO & Director Theodore Hanson), adding the rebrand is intended to support “a more integrated operating model focused on higher-value solutions and deeper client relationships” and to “increase our cross-selling opportunities.”

  • Hanson said the company is changing commercial segment reporting “by industry rather than mode of delivery” and will disclose “our commercial consulting book-to-bill,” describing the change as aligned with its “Next Wave Growth Strategy and industry-led approach.”

  • On Q1 performance, Hanson highlighted “Revenues for the first quarter were $968.3 million” and pointed to Commercial demand in “AI and data, cloud and infrastructure and application engineering and modernization,” while noting “adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.6% was below our expectations for the quarter,” driven “largely by business mix.”

  • On Federal, Hanson reported “Federal segment new contract awards totaled $151.3 million” and said “Federal contract backlog was approximately $2.8 billion at quarter end,” while flagging that the company “experienced some funding delays at the Department of Homeland Security, which is navigating both a shutdown and a leadership transition.”

  • Hanson also tied near-term execution to leadership and M&A actions: “We welcome Ashish Jandial as President of Commercial North America; Sanjita Singh as President of India and International; and Donnie Scott as President of our Federal Government segment,” and “We also successfully closed the acquisition of Quinnox.”

  • “For the first quarter, revenues totaled $968.3 million,” said (Executive VP & CFO Marie Perry), adding “Quinnox contributed less than 1 month to the quarterly results,” while “SG&A expenses included $12.8 million in acquisition, integration and strategic planning expenses that were not included in our previously announced guidance estimates.”

Outlook

  • “For the second quarter of 2026, we are estimating revenues of $970 million to $1 billion, net income of $8 million to $13.7 million, adjusted EBITDA of $85 million to $95 million and adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.8% to 9.5%,” said (CFO Perry), with the guidance assuming “no further deterioration in the markets that we serve.”

  • Perry said Q2 includes “$8 million to $10 million in strategic planning expenses related to the execution of our Next Wave Growth Strategy, which we expect will decline over the coming quarters,” alongside “targeted initiatives that will generate meaningful structural cost savings for the business.”

  • On commercial demand and conversion dynamics into Q2, (President Sadasivam Iyer) said “sales cycles are getting slightly longer as in clients are deliberating longer before they pull the trigger on projects,” and added “some of that has been factored into the guide for Q2.”

Financial Results

  • Perry reported Q1 revenue of $968.3 million, including Commercial segment revenue of $675.5 million and Federal Government segment revenue of $292.8 million.

  • Margin and profitability items disclosed included gross margin of 27.5%, Commercial segment gross margin of 31%, Federal segment gross margin of 19.6%, net income of $5.5 million, adjusted EBITDA of $83.6 million, and adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.6%.

  • Perry said the margin pressure included mix plus “headwinds from changes in our foreign exchange rate related to our delivery center in Mexico,” and noted the quarter’s effective tax rate was 48.1% “reflecting the onetime discrete items not included in our guidance.”

  • Capital allocation and leverage items included the Quinnox purchase price of $290 million, share repurchases of $39 million for 0.8 million shares at an average price of $47.69, $934 million remaining under the $1 billion authorization, cash and cash equivalents of $143.6 million, and a net leverage ratio of 3.1x, with Perry stating: “We are committed to reducing our debt over time in order to bring our net leverage ratio closer to 2.5x target.”

  • Perry reported free cash flow of $9.1 million and said it was pressured “primarily due to an increase in DSO,” while (CEO Hanson) added: “No change in behavior, no increase in bad debt.”

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets: asked about the “lower-than-expected contribution from some higher-margin commercial solutions” and why visibility broke down; (CEO Hanson) said “the ramp-up of higher-margin solutions, especially in our enterprise software areas was slower and later into the quarter,” adding “it’s a gross margin issue. It’s not an expense issue.”

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets: pressed on whether enterprise-software softness is “structural”; (CEO Hanson) cited “record bookings” exiting Q4 in “Workday… ServiceNow and Salesforce” but said “we just didn’t see the conversion to revenue at historical rates,” calling it “temporary” and pointing to Q2 margin guidance “solely on the back of improving gross margins.”

  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: asked about financial services decline implications; (President Iyer) said big banks showed “continued tight management of expenditure,” while “we are seeing some green shoots in insurance and also some green shoots in diversified financials.”

  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: asked about the 1.1x commercial consulting book-to-bill and deal duration; (President Iyer) said strength was “relatively broad-based,” with “longer-term bookings in cybersecurity” and “overall durations are shifting rightward and lengthening because of some of the cloud and infrastructure work.”

  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: asked about the $12.8 million unanticipated expenses and Quinnox contribution; (CFO Perry) said the costs relate to implementing the multi-year savings effort and included “Quinnox… our go-to-market, our back office outsourcing and then our ERP,” and on Quinnox’s model she reiterated “$100 million” revenue contribution framework with “EBITDA margin of low 20%.”

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: asked about assignment trends; (CEO Hanson) said Q4-to-Q1 was “down kind of mid-single digits, low to mid-single digits… seasonally about what we see every year,” with “Pay-to-bill margins pretty steady.”

  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: asked about federal budget implications; (CEO Hanson) said DHS is a “watch item,” but emphasized defense funding and that “AI… Data… Cybersecurity” should be key areas, adding “we’re expecting… a better second quarter than first quarter for sure.”

  • Jason Haas, Wells Fargo: asked about staffing “green shoots” and whether slowed high-margin work “push[ed] into 2Q”; (CEO Hanson) said IT staffing “really tracks IT spending,” while (President Iyer) said the issue was slower conversion and “some of that has been factored into the guide for Q2.”

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: asked about pricing pressure and DSO; (President Iyer) said “we’re not seeing a material compression in pricing,” describing the margin issue as “really timing,” while (CFO Perry) said free cash flow conversion is still “a good rule of thumb… 60% of our adjusted EBITDA converting.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing around margin drivers, enterprise platform conversion, and cash flow timing, including Silber’s challenge on structural risk and Marcon’s pricing-pressure questions.

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive on strategic actions (rebrand, leadership, Quinnox) but more defensive/explanatory in Q&A on margins and conversion timing, with Hanson emphasizing “It’s a gross margin issue. It’s not an expense issue.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s more upbeat framing (record bookings and stronger margins), the current call’s tone shifted toward explaining execution timing and mix, while maintaining confidence via Q2 margin guidance and commentary that the softness was “temporary.”

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Strategic execution advanced from “soon to be Everforth” (prior call) to a definitive go-live: “on Friday… operating as Everforth and trading under… EFOR,” and the Quinnox deal moved from “intent to acquire” to “successfully closed.”

  • Profitability discussion shifted from Q4’s “Adjusted EBITDA margin was 11%” to Q1’s “adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.6% was below our expectations,” with management attributing the change to mix and timing rather than pricing.

  • Commercial bookings commentary cooled from Q4’s “record $444.4 million… book-to-bill of 1.3x” to Q1’s trailing 12-month commercial consulting book-to-bill of 1.1x, and Q&A concentrated on conversion from enterprise-platform bookings into revenue.

  • Federal narrative moved from shutdown-delayed awards and “pent-up demand in Q1” (prior call) to “award activity… pick up” post-budget passage, while introducing specific DHS funding delays tied to “a shutdown and a leadership transition.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Hanson cited macro uncertainty: clients face “a very volatile macro environment with continued uncertainty around how technologies such as AI and enterprise software will ultimately impact the technology landscape and influence their IT spending,” which management linked to “near-term variability.”

  • Federal risk was framed around DHS: “funding delays at the Department of Homeland Security,” with management positioning defense, intelligence, and national security as better-funded areas.

  • Margin risk was tied to mix and conversion timing in higher-margin enterprise platforms; management’s mitigation was “disciplined expense management,” structural cost savings initiatives, and guidance that embeds a higher Q2 margin range.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a transition quarter into the Everforth brand and operating model, while acknowledging a margin shortfall tied to slower enterprise-platform revenue conversion and a less favorable mix. Leadership additions and the closed Quinnox acquisition were positioned as building blocks for higher-value solutions and offshore delivery scale, while Q2 guidance called for $970 million to $1 billion of revenue and an adjusted EBITDA margin of 8.8% to 9.5%, supported by improving gross margin, ongoing cost actions, and moderating strategic-planning expenses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Booz Allen sinks as Bessent says Treasury has lost confidence in the company for government contracts
data.description:
Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH) closed down 4.5% on Wednesday after Treasury Secretary Bessent told a U.S. Senate hearing that the government contractor had lost his agency’s confidence.

Bessent said the loss of confidence in Booz Allen (BAH) led to the agency’s decision to cancel its contracts with the consulting firm earlier this year, in a surprise move that came years after a former employee leaked tax returns to media outlets.

"We no longer have confidence in that firm’s ability to screen, vet, and deploy contractors within the IRS or indeed the entire Treasury Department," Bessent said during a Senate Appropriations Financial Services and General Government Subcommittee hearing as he explained the agency's decision to cancel its contracts with the company.

When the Treasury Department canceled ~$21M in contracts, it said the decision stemmed from deficiencies in data security controls tied to Booz Allen's (BAH) work with the Internal Revenue Service.

Booz Allen (BAH) shares have dropped 24% since the cancellation.

data.title: Century Communities outlines 2026 deliveries of 9,500 to 10,500 amid reduced guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Century Communities (CCS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Executive chairman Dale Francescon said macro conditions “intensified even further beginning in early March,” citing “geopolitical issues and increased economic uncertainties, coupled with higher interest rates and gas prices” that “weighed on our order activity most meaningfully in March.” He highlighted balance-sheet and operational actions, including that the company “repurchased approximately 2% of our shares outstanding at the beginning of the year at a 27% discount to our book value” and “increased our quarterly cash dividend by 10% to $0.32 per share.”

  • CEO Robert Francescon described a strategy shift from Q4 to Q1: “While in the fourth quarter of last year, we focused more on pace versus price, we took the more balanced approach in the first quarter 2026 that we outlined on our conference call last quarter.” He said March demand softened year-over-year and the quarter ended with “net new orders totaling 2,379 homes,” while “our cancellation rate of 12.2% in the first quarter was below the levels we experienced throughout most of 2025.”

  • Chief Financial Officer John Dixon reported profitability and capital returns, stating, “In the first quarter, pretax income was $33 million and net income was $24 million or $0.84 per diluted share,” adding, “Adjusted net income was $26 million or $0.88 per diluted share.”

Outlook

  • CFO John Dixon reduced full-year targets, saying, “We are reducing our full year 2026 home delivery guidance by 5% and now expect it to be in the range of 9,500 to 10,500 homes, and our home sales revenues to be in the range of $3.5 billion to $3.8 billion.”

  • CEO Robert Francescon framed near-term sales conditions as volatile but improving from March, stating, “Our order activity so far in April has trended better than March with orders also improving sequentially over the past several weeks.”

  • On incentives and margins into Q2, CEO Francescon said, “Assuming current market conditions, we expect incentives on closed homes in the second quarter of 2026 to be similar with first quarter levels,” while CFO Dixon added that in Q2 “the most significant driver of our adjusted homebuilding gross margin [will] continue to be incentives needed to generate an acceptable sales pace.”

Financial results

  • CFO John Dixon said, “Home sales revenues for the first quarter were $734 million with our average sales price of $365,000,” and noted total deliveries of “2,013 homes during the first quarter.”

  • On profitability drivers, CFO Dixon said, “Our first quarter 2026 GAAP homebuilding gross margin of 17.8% increased by 240 basis points over fourth quarter 2025 margins of 15.4%,” and added, “Our adjusted gross margin in the first quarter was 19.7% compared to 18.3% in the fourth quarter of 2025.” He also disclosed a one-time margin benefit: “Our first quarter margin benefited by 90 basis points from a reduction to our warranty accrual and rebate collections in excess of previous estimates.”

  • CFO Dixon described cost and overhead dynamics: “SG&A as a percentage of home sales revenue was 15.8% in the first quarter and impacted by lower-than-expected deliveries,” and said the company expects “SG&A as a percentage of home sales revenue of 14.5% for the second quarter.”

  • CFO Dixon highlighted segment contribution and balance sheet, saying, “Revenues from financial services were $22 million in the first quarter, and the business generated pretax income of $8 million,” and added, “We ended the quarter with $2.6 billion in stockholders' equity and $886 million of liquidity.”

Q&A

  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital Securities: asked about competitors’ spec inventory adjustments and broad spec levels; CFO Dixon replied, “we're comfortable with most markets with where the overall finished spec inventory is at,” adding the focus is “to really ensure at a community level” the company is strong on “pricing as well as consumer demand.”

  • Alexander Rygiel, Texas Capital Securities: asked when geographic expansion could reaccelerate; CEO Robert Francescon said, “We're now in over 45 markets coast to coast,” and added, “our biggest focus is growing within our existing footprint,” targeting “a top 10… or… top 5 position” in markets.

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked about vendor fuel surcharges and cost inflation risk; CEO Francescon said, “to date, we've been able to avoid price increases,” adding, “Is that something that's going to be a topic in Q3 and Q4? Don't know.”

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked about land sale detail and pipeline; CEO Francescon said it was “just an opportunistic item” involving “back half lots that we did not need for the foreseeable future.”

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP: asked which regions were more affected and what April shows; CEO Francescon said, “the Southeast still remains really strong,” cited “green shoots” in Austin, and said “the Bay Area has probably been the slowest or the weakest market.”

  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: asked how April is trending and whether incentives can stay flat; CEO Francescon said April is “better than March,” and on incentives, “it’s always fluid,” but “we feel fairly comfortable… we will be flat at worst from where we were in Q1 to where we'll be in Q2.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, with multiple questions probing whether macro uncertainty is driving deeper incentive pressure and whether regional softness (notably the Bay Area) could worsen.

  • Management’s tone was slightly cautious but steady, repeatedly tying weaker March demand to external shocks; CEO Robert Francescon emphasized near-term improvement with “April has actually started out better than March,” while also acknowledging uncertainty with “it’s always fluid.”

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from optimism around a “balanced approach” entering 2026 to more explicit macro-driven caution and a guidance reduction tied to “the conflict in the Middle East” and “higher interest rates and gas prices.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance direction changed: in Q4 2025, CFO Dixon guided 2026 deliveries of “10,000 to 11,000 homes” and revenue of “$3.6 billion to $4.1 billion,” while in Q1 2026 he lowered them to “9,500 to 10,500 homes” and “$3.5 billion to $3.8 billion.”

  • Incentives commentary remained central, but the near-term trajectory tightened; Q4 anticipated Q1 incentives “improve by up to 50 basis points,” while Q1 said Q2 incentives are expected to be “similar with first quarter levels.”

  • Capital return actions increased in Q1, with a dividend raise to $0.32 and $40 million of buybacks, versus Q4’s $0.29 dividend level and $20 million of buybacks.

Risks and concerns

  • Executive chairman Dale Francescon cited “geopolitical issues,” “increased economic uncertainties,” “higher interest rates and gas prices,” and said these factors “further eroded consumer settlement.”

  • Incentive dependence was framed as a margin risk; CFO John Dixon said the “most significant driver” of adjusted gross margin remains “incentives needed to generate an acceptable sales pace.”

  • Cost inflation risk from fuel was raised by analysts; CEO Robert Francescon said, “there's a lot of headlines on oil and petroleum products,” but added, “to date, we've been able to avoid price increases.”

Final takeaway

Management attributed a March demand step-down to geopolitical and macro uncertainty, then lowered full-year 2026 delivery and revenue guidance while maintaining a “balance pace and price” operating stance. The company emphasized inventory control, flexible land options, and shareholder returns (higher dividend and continued buybacks), while signaling that near-term profitability remains highly sensitive to incentive levels and interest-rate-driven affordability conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Raymond James outlines ~$2.3B noncomp expense target while expecting ~1% Q3 fee growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Raymond James Financial (RJF) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Paul Shoukry said Raymond James “delivered strong results this quarter despite a challenging and volatile market environment,” and highlighted “record quarterly revenues of $3.86 billion” and “pretax income of $735 million.”
  • CEO Shoukry emphasized adviser growth and recruiting momentum, including “quarterly domestic net new assets…$23 billion” and recruiting with “trailing 12-month production totaling $141 million” and “nearly $21 billion of client assets at their previous firms.”
  • CEO Shoukry said the firm will keep investing “in automation, process improvement and AI as part of our more than $1.1 billion annual technology spend,” and described an “AI operations agent” that “has been rolled out to a few hundred advisers.”
  • CEO Shoukry said capital markets improved “primarily driven by stronger investment banking revenues with a particularly strong performance in the month of March,” and added, “We entered this third quarter with a robust pipeline.”
  • CFO Jonathan Oorlog said, “Net income available to common shareholders was $542 million with earnings per diluted share of $2.72,” and added that “adjusted earnings per diluted share” were “$2.83” and “adjusted pretax margin was 19.7%.”

Outlook

  • CFO Oorlog provided segment-level expectations, stating, “we expect fiscal third quarter 2026 asset management and related administrative fees, to be higher by approximately 1% over the second quarter level.”
  • CFO Oorlog added interest-related guidance, saying, “we would expect the aggregate of NII and RJBDP third-party fees in the third quarter to be up approximately 1% from the second quarter level,” while cautioning that “there are many variables which could influence actual results including any interest rate actions.”
  • CFO Oorlog reiterated the full-year cost framework: “For the fiscal year, we remain on track with our target level of noncompensation expenses of approximately $2.3 billion,” and the tax framework: “we continue to estimate our effective tax rate for fiscal 2026 to be approximately 24% to 25%.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s forward-looking focus shifted from “two fewer billing days” and an expected Q2 decline in combined NII/RJBDP to billing-day tailwinds in Q3 (one additional day) supporting both fee and NII/RJBDP expectations.

Financial Results

  • CFO Oorlog reported firmwide results: record “net revenues of $3.86 billion,” “net income…$542 million,” and “earnings per diluted share of $2.72,” with adjusted EPS of $2.83.
  • CFO Oorlog reported Private Client Group pretax income of $416 million on “record quarterly net revenues of $2.81 billion,” and said pretax income “declined 3% year-over-year” due to “interest rate reductions…which reduced our noncompensable revenues.”
  • CFO Oorlog said Capital Markets produced “quarterly net revenues of $464 million and a pretax income of $51 million,” attributing growth to “higher debt and equity underwriting revenues as well as higher M&A and advisory revenues.”
  • CFO Oorlog reported Asset Management pretax income of $137 million on “record net revenues of $327 million,” tied to “strong net inflows into PCG fee-based accounts.”
  • CFO Oorlog reported Bank segment “net revenues of $486 million and pretax income of $166 million,” and said “incremental interest revenues were nearly offset” by “2 fewer interest earning days” and “a full quarter impact of interest rate cuts.”
  • CFO Oorlog reported cash and balance sheet details including “clients' domestic cash sweep and enhanced savings program balances…$57.8 billion” and “combined net interest income and RJBDP fees…declined…to $650 million,” alongside “record bank loans of $54.8 billion.”

Q&A

  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: asked about PCG competition, potential M&A-driven adviser movement, and PCG pretax yield pressure; CEO Paul Shoukry responded that confidence is driven by “the volume of home office visits,” “an uptick of commits,” and noted private equity is competitive, adding he has “heard that there's at least 1 or 2 firms that have tried to raise capital…that weren't able to do so,” while attributing margin pressure to “short-term rates…down” and higher recruiting spend.
  • Devin Ryan, Citizens JMP: asked about AI as a net positive and “Agentic” cash sweep optimization risk and potential platform fees; CEO Shoukry said the tool is “conceptual,” argued sweep declines have already occurred as advisers move clients to alternatives, and stated, “we would be flexible and open to evolving” pricing if industry dynamics change.
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan: asked about the $1.1 billion technology spend priorities and how AI success is measured, plus capital markets pipeline visibility; CEO Shoukry said “the vast majority…is being focused on the Private Client Group,” described adviser feedback via a “Technology Advisory Council,” and said March was “stronger than we expected” with “engagement letters…very promising,” while emphasizing timing uncertainty.
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked about AI-driven margin upside and odds of larger wealth M&A; CEO Shoukry said it is “hard to dimension the actual margin impact at this juncture,” and described Raymond James’ approach: “We don't really do takeovers. We invite other firms to the Raymond James family.”
  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: pressed on PCG comp ratio rising despite revenue growth; CEO Shoukry cited mix shift toward independents (higher payouts) and “tiered payout systems.”
  • Steven Chubak, Wolfe: asked whether PCG mix headwinds persist and revisited cash economics risk and fee-based alternatives; CEO Shoukry said there is “pretty good uptick in the employee affiliation option,” and reiterated flexibility on pricing while prioritizing “competitive and fair” economics.
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: asked how RJ deepens adviser relationships and whether new advisers behave differently, plus SBL distribution differences; CEO Shoukry emphasized holistic capabilities (tech, products, bank, insurance) and said new advisers are often “more blown away,” while noting no major behavior differences.
  • James Mitchell, Seaport: asked what is driving SBL acceleration and TriState deposit economics; CEO Shoukry said SBL growth has been “almost identical between the 2” platforms at a “30-ish percent rate,” and called TriState “a contributor on all fronts.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical on profitability drivers and cash economics, with questions framing “operating leverage negative” (BMO) and that “the market is ascribing a lower terminal value to cash derive profits” (Wolfe).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and steady, repeatedly emphasizing confidence while avoiding hard commitments on AI margin impact, with CEO Shoukry saying AI margin specificity would be “arbitrary and too preliminary.”
  • Versus last quarter, the Q&A leaned more into AI disruption and cash monetization mechanics, while last quarter’s Q&A more heavily emphasized net new assets sustainability, deposit mix, and capital markets recovery timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reported sequential improvement in consolidated results versus Q1, with Q2 described as “record quarterly revenues,” while Q1 had already been “record quarterly revenues” at a lower level.
  • Capital Markets messaging shifted from Q1: “results declined this quarter” to Q2: “results improved this quarter,” with both quarters citing a “robust pipeline” but Q2 pointing to a “particularly strong…March.”
  • PCG net new assets moderated from “nearly $31 billion” in Q1 to “$23 billion” in Q2, while CEO Shoukry maintained that recruiting and retention remained “robust” and pointed to “strong retention and continued recruiting momentum.”
  • Interest-rate headwinds remained a consistent theme, but Q2 provided more detail on combined NII/RJBDP declining to $650 million and framed Q3 as modestly higher assuming static rates.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Shoukry cited “challenging and volatile market” conditions and highlighted that investment banking conversion timing remains uncertain: “we don't know when those pipelines will convert to revenues.”
  • CFO Oorlog flagged sensitivity to rates and balances, stating “many variables…including any interest rate actions,” and described cash sweep balances declining seasonally due to “record quarterly fee billings…approximately $1.9 billion” and tax activity.
  • Management acknowledged margin pressure drivers in PCG, with CEO Shoukry attributing it to “short-term rates…down” and recruiting investment, and explaining mix shifts toward independent advisers can raise payout ratios.

Final Takeaway

Raymond James leadership framed Q2 as a record-revenue quarter supported by adviser recruiting, fee-based asset growth, and improved capital markets performance, while positioning near-term outlook around modest sequential increases in Q3 asset management fees and combined NII/RJBDP (both guided to roughly +1%) and maintaining a full-year noncompensation expense target of about $2.3 billion. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on PCG margin dynamics, the durability of cash economics under “Agentic” AI scenarios, and the timing risk embedded in investment banking pipelines, while management emphasized pricing flexibility, continued tech investment, and uncertainty on quantifying AI-driven margin benefits today.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: LVS targets $700M Macao quarterly EBITDA while planning Venetian refresh through end of 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Las Vegas Sands (LVS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Our strategic priorities remain clear and consistent with the goals of investing with discipline and creating meaningful shareholder returns,” said (Chairman, CEO, President & Treasurer Patrick Dumont).

  • Dumont highlighted Singapore profitability: “We once again delivered outstanding financial results of Marina Bay Sands in Singapore with EBITDA increasing over 30% to reach $788 million,” adding that “Singapore is an ideal market for high-value tourism spending.”

  • On Macao, Dumont said, “We delivered $633 million in EBITDA for the quarter, an increase of over 18%,” and noted, “Mass market revenue share reached 25.7% for this quarter, our strongest performance since the first quarter of 2024.”

  • Dumont framed an operating and investment pivot toward service and room product in Macao: “We are making progress,” but “implementing meaningful improvements in the service pillar of our strategy in Macao will be critical,” including “targeted investments in training and hiring of additional customer-focused team members throughout the portfolio.”

  • Dumont set renovation timing and emphasized limited disruption: “We will begin with the Venetian where work is already in progress with refreshed room products beginning to come into service in the third quarter of 2026,” and “Additional luxurious suite product and the total product refresh is targeted to be completed by the end of 2027.”

  • Capital return was a central prepared-remarks item: “We repurchased $740 million of LVS stock during the quarter,” and “paid our recurring quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share,” said (CEO Dumont).

  • CFO remarks were not included in this transcript; financial commentary in prepared remarks was delivered by Dumont.

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal quarterly EPS or revenue guidance on the call, and did not give a quantified outlook framework tied to analyst estimates.

  • Dumont reiterated a Macao earnings ambition: “We have a goal of reaching $700 million in quarterly EBITDA and beyond over time as we fully implement our investment and operating strategies and as the Macao market continues to grow.”

  • For Singapore’s IR2, Dumont emphasized a return hurdle: “We’ve always said that you kind of target a 20% return,” and added, “we’re looking at a total project return in excess of the 20% we talked about.”

  • On Macao cost and margin direction while building service, Dumont warned, “Our investments in improving service offerings will naturally increase expenses, which will continue to negatively impact margins as we implement our strategy,” while adding, “We do expect margins to improve over time as we grow revenue.”

Financial results

  • Dumont reported property-level EBITDA and key adjustments: “Macao EBITDA was $633 million,” and “If we had held as expected in our rolling program, our EBITDA would have been lower by $15 million.”

  • Dumont described Macao margins under a hold-adjusted lens: “When adjusted for a higher-than-expected hold in the rolling segment, our EBITDA margin for the Macao portfolio of properties would have been 29.6%.”

  • For Macao property margins, Dumont said, “Margin for the quarter at the Venetian was 33.5%, while margin at the Londoner was 29.6%.”

  • For Singapore, Dumont reported: “Marina Bay Sands EBITDA for the quarter was $788 million at a margin of 53%,” and added, “If we had held as expected in our rolling program, our EBITDA would have been higher by $6 million.”

  • On capital returns and ownership, Dumont said, “We have now purchased 14.3% of the company’s outstanding shares over the last 10 quarters,” and “The company’s ownership of SCL remained at 74.8% as of March 31, 2026.”

Q&A

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked what was driving “$18 billion of rolling chips” and whether geopolitical shifts were helping Singapore; (CEO Dumont) responded, “the Marina Bay Sands growth story… is really a story about investment,” while cautioning that “VIP is a very volatile segment… these quarters can be highly concentrated and can vary.”

  • Daniel Politzer, JPMorgan: asked what market growth is needed to reach Macao’s $700 million quarterly EBITDA; (CEO Dumont) answered, “that’s a milestone that’s achievable,” but “it’s going to require us to continue on the execution of hospitality and service,” and (President, CEO & Executive Director Kwan Chum) added, “the market continues to grow… We had 14% growth year-on-year this quarter,” and “we gained share in every single segment.”

  • Brandt Montour, Barclays: probed Singapore VIP hold dynamics and “reversion away from side bets”; (CEO Dumont) said the quarter’s VIP mix “was a barbell,” combining “traditional bets in a very conservative manner” and patrons who “really enjoy the volatility and side bets.”

  • Robin Farley, UBS: asked whether IR2 targets VIP or broader demand and about incremental EBITDA; (CEO Dumont) said, “this will be the most luxurious and most highly amenity hotel in the world,” and indicated the goal is smoothing volatility with “more inventory,” while anchoring the investment to “a total project return in excess of the 20%.”

  • Elizabeth Dove, Goldman Sachs: asked if the stepped-up buyback is an “appropriate… run rate” and about Macao margin resilience; (CEO Dumont) said, “we were a little more aggressive than maybe you’ve seen in prior quarters,” and (President Chum) said service investments “are having an impact on the cost structure,” while “over time, we intend to grow margin as the revenue levels continue to increase.”

  • John DeCree, CBRE: asked whether Macao service hiring is fixed or variable and timing; (CEO Dumont) said, “These are hires that are designed to increase and enhance the service levels,” and “A significant number have actually… are actually in the OpEx now… and it will continue to be added over the next couple of quarters.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical on sustainability and drivers, with repeated probing on Singapore’s unusually large VIP volumes/hold behavior and Macao’s path to $700 million EBITDA; examples include questions on “astronomical levels” (JPMorgan) and whether $600 million is the right “run rate” (Stifel).

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was positive on Singapore and execution-led, while more cautionary on Macao margins; Dumont balanced confidence (“We are confident in our strategy in Macao”) with cost tradeoffs (“will continue to negatively impact margins”).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s language shifted toward operational specifics on Macao reinvestment (training/hiring and Venetian room timing) and away from Q4’s leadership transition framing; the prior call emphasized “Rob is going to be serving in a new role as Senior Adviser… for the next 2 years,” while Q1 centered on execution under Dumont.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Singapore EBITDA moved from $806 million in Q4 2025 to $788 million in Q1 2026, while management emphasized ongoing volatility in VIP play in Q1 (“VIP is a very volatile segment”) versus Q4’s emphasis that Marina Bay Sands “defies the seasonality.”

  • Macao EBITDA increased from $608 million in Q4 2025 to $633 million in Q1 2026, while Q1 introduced more explicit near-term margin pressure from service investment (“will continue to negatively impact margins”) compared with Q4’s positioning of Macao as a “low 30% margin business.”

  • Capital returns accelerated: Q4 disclosed “We repurchased $500 million of LVS stock” and a $0.25 dividend, while Q1 disclosed “We repurchased $740 million” and a $0.30 dividend.

Risks and concerns

  • Dumont flagged Macao competitive intensity and margin pressure: “The competition in [the premium] segment remains intense,” and “our investments… will naturally increase expenses,” which “will continue to negatively impact margins as we implement our strategy.”

  • Management highlighted concentration/volatility risk in Singapore VIP: “VIP is a very volatile segment… it could be concentrated at times… and they can vary from quarter-to-quarter,” said (CEO Dumont).

  • On renovation execution risk, management argued disruption will be limited: “The work we envision will not create significant disruption,” said (CEO Dumont), and (President Chum) added, “we don’t expect meaningful disruption impact… new rooms will continuously be coming back… starting from the third quarter.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 around continued strength at Marina Bay Sands, a stepped-up capital return program, and a Macao plan centered on service hiring and product refresh to pursue “$700 million in quarterly EBITDA and beyond over time,” while acknowledging that service investments are expected to pressure margins near term and that VIP performance—especially in Singapore—can be “highly concentrated and can vary.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Water Works, Essential Utilities merger approved by Kentucky regulator
data.description:
American Water Works (AWK) and Essential Utilities (WTRG) said Wednesday that the Kentucky Public Service Commission approved the companies' proposed merger.

The Kentucky PSC's approval follows the merger's approval by shareholders of both companies at their respective special shareholder meetings held in February.

The all-stock transaction, announced in October, will create a combined company serving more than 4.7M water and wastewater customer connections and more than 740K gas customer connections across 17 states and on 18 military installations.

The combined company has said it expects to maintain American Water's (AWK) 7%-9% earnings per share and dividend growth targets.

The company will operate under the American Water (AWK) name and be headquartered in Camden, New Jersey.

data.title: Navy Secretary John Phelan ousted amid clash with Pentagon leadership
data.description:
John Phelan has been removed as secretary of the Navy following months of internal friction with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, according to U.S. officials familiar with the matter.

The leadership change was disclosed in a social media statement from Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell, who said Hegseth and Deputy Defense Secretary Steve Feinberg were “grateful to Secretary Phelan for his service to the Department and the United States Navy.”

Parnell said Hung Cao, the current undersecretary of the Navy and a military veteran, will assume the role on an acting basis. Cao was the Republican nominee in Virginia’s 2024 Senate race, where he was defeated by Democratic Sen. Tim Kaine.

Phelan’s departure comes as U.S. forces are carrying out a large naval containment effort around Iranian ports, with more than 15 American warships operating in the Middle East region.

Officials said Phelan’s tenure had been marked by a strained relationship with Hegseth and Feinberg, including resentment over Phelan’s direct access to President Donald Trump, The Wall Street Journal reported.

Phelan, who lives near Trump’s Mar-a-Lago property in Florida, was known to speak regularly with the president and previously told lawmakers he exchanged late-night messages with Trump about naval shipbuilding.

According to people familiar with internal discussions, tensions worsened last fall after Phelan proposed a new battleship concept directly to Trump without first going through Hegseth, the Journal reported.

In the months that followed, Pentagon leadership moved to reduce Phelan’s influence, officials said, including the creation of a new submarine acquisition overseer reporting directly to Feinberg rather than through traditional Navy channels.

The episode adds to broader turmoil within the Pentagon. Hegseth has dismissed nearly two dozen senior military officers during his tenure, including Army Chief of Staff Gen. Randy George, and has also been at odds with senior Army leadership.

The removal of Phelan comes during the Navy’s largest annual conference, where he had spent recent days outlining service priorities to defense contractors and journalists. It also follows the Pentagon’s release of its latest budget proposal to Congress, which includes $65.8 billion earmarked for shipbuilding.

data.title: Crown Castle reiterates 2026 outlook with ~$1B buyback plan and $65M annualized cost reduction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Crown Castle (CCI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered solid first quarter results and are reiterating our guidance for full year 2026." (CEO, President & Director Christian Hillabrant)

  • "This is a transformative year for Crown Castle, and we believe we have an opportunity to generate attractive shareholder returns as we transition to a stand-alone tower business and pursue our goal of becoming a best-in-class U.S. tower operator." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "Our first priority is to conclude the sale of our small cell and fiber businesses, which we believe remains on track to close in the first half of 2026." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "After DISH defaulted on its payment obligations in January, we exercised our right to terminate the agreement, and we are seeking to recover the remaining payments DISH owed for the terms of the contract." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "During the first quarter, we amended our pending litigation against DISH to include a claim for breach of contract alongside our request for declaratory judgment." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "In the first quarter, we successfully executed a restructuring of our tower and corporate organizations, resulting in an anticipated $65 million reduction to annualized run rate cost." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "First quarter organic growth, excluding the impact of Sprint cancellations and DISH terminations, was 3.1% or $30 million" and was "more than offset at site rental revenues by $5 million of Sprint cancellations, $49 million of DISH terminations and a $26 million decrease in noncash straight-line revenues and amortization of prepaid rent." (Executive VP & CFO Sunit Patel)

Outlook

  • "Our full year outlook remains unchanged." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "At the midpoint of the range for full year 2026, we expect site rental revenues of approximately $3.9 billion, adjusted EBITDA of approximately $2.7 billion and AFFO of approximately $1.9 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "As a reminder, for the purposes of building our full year 2026 outlook, we'll assume the sale of the small cell and fiber businesses closes on June 30." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "Following the close of the transaction, we plan to allocate approximately $1 billion to share repurchases and approximately $7 billion to repay debt, allowing us to remain at our target leverage range between 6x and 6.5x." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "Our full year 2026 outlook positions us well to meet our unchanged range for AFFO for the 12 months following the anticipated close of the transaction of $2.1 billion at the midpoint." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA and AFFO in the first quarter benefited from lower repair and maintenance costs, sustaining capital expenditures and other nonlabor costs" but "these lower costs were largely due to timing and seasonality, so we expect them to occur later in the year." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "We also experienced a modest decrease in quarterly interest expense due to lower-than-anticipated short-term borrowing rates." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "Lastly, our outlook for discretionary CapEx remains unchanged at $200 million or $160 million, net of $40 million of prepaid rent received." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

Q&A

  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked about potentially splitting the fiber/small cell transaction approvals to accelerate closing; CEO Hillabrant: "we remain extremely confident that we will close by the end of first half or as soon as possible."

  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked whether CCI’s medium/long-term growth should differ from peers; CEO Hillabrant: "nothing systemically" and the difference has been "the timing of when that growth occurred," adding the portfolio skews "towards urban and suburban" which can drive "earlier in the cycle" activity.

  • Matthew Niknam, Truist: asked about 5G densification and whether satellite partnerships affect planning; CEO Hillabrant: densification behavior is "not really any change from past deployments" and satellite impact is "de minimis or inconsequential at this point."

  • Aryeh Klein, BMO: asked where benchmarking vs peers can drive improvement; CEO Hillabrant: the goal is improving customer experience so that "when there's choice of a customer between 2 tower companies that we win 100% of the jump balls."

  • Richard Choe, JPMorgan: asked about timing and meaning of edge compute; CEO Hillabrant: "I would characterize this in the trial phase" and said the company is pursuing it "with very little capital required," while noting most sites have fiber backhaul.

  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: asked about new tower builds and margin opportunity; CEO Hillabrant: "I certainly don't want to raise expectations that we're going into a mass tower build here" and it will be "small volumes" with a "very disciplined approach"; CFO Patel: beyond the staffing reduction, CCI aims to buy land under towers and invest in automation, saying by 2030 it can deliver "well over 200 basis points in margin improvements."

  • Nicholas Del Deo, MoffettNathanson: asked about land ownership targets and CapEx trajectory; CFO Patel: "our goal is over the next handful of years, get to a point where we own from 30% to as much as 40% of the land underneath our towers" and "this year's guide should be adequate."

  • Brandon Nispel, KeyBanc: challenged the dividend payout level and suggested shifting to buybacks; CFO Patel: the company decided "to basically reiterate that the dividend would stay where it is now" while paying down debt to "remain investment grade" and executing "$1 billion of shares" repurchases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing around execution and capital allocation, with repeated probing on closing timing, dividend policy, and potential technology threats (e.g., fixed wireless vs satellite, fiber-to-the-home), including KeyBanc’s framing that a dividend cut could be "more accretive" alongside buybacks.

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and steady in prepared remarks and generally confident in answers, repeatedly using firm language on key items such as closing confidence ("extremely confident") and DISH enforcement ("We believe we have a strong legal case against DISH" (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)), while avoiding precise timing on uncertain items like litigation outcomes ("legal outcome is going to take at least a year" (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)).

  • Versus last quarter, management maintained the same core messages (asset-sale timing, DISH enforcement, and cost actions) but added more detail on the DISH litigation posture (including "a claim against EchoStar") and framed edge compute explicitly as a "trial phase." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s strategic priorities were consistent quarter over quarter: completing the small cell and fiber sale in 1H 2026, defending value from the DISH agreement, and reshaping the organization to run a U.S.-only tower business.

  • New in the current quarter was added specificity on legal escalation: management said it "amended" the DISH litigation to include "breach of contract" and a claim involving EchoStar, versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on seeking to recover "in excess of $3.5 billion" and supporting spectrum being put to work while enforcing contract rights.

  • The tone in analyst questioning broadened from Q4’s concentration on DISH mechanics and transaction approvals to Q1’s heavier focus on medium-term growth drivers (5G densification, 2027+ spectrum auctions, edge compute monetization), plus more pointed dividend framework debate.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We are seeking to recover the remaining payments DISH owed" and management cautioned on uncertainty and duration, stating "legal outcome is going to take at least a year" and timing "is still very much an unknown." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • Management highlighted cost timing risk in quarterly results: Q1 cost benefits were "largely due to timing and seasonality" and are expected later in the year. (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • On potential technology substitution, management repeatedly framed satellite as complementary and not currently material to the tower business, saying impact is "de minimis or inconsequential at this point." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

Final Takeaway

Management reiterated full-year 2026 guidance while positioning 2026 as a transition to a “stand-alone tower business,” anchored by closing the small cell and fiber sale in 1H 2026, enforcing claims tied to DISH’s default (including updated litigation that adds breach of contract claims and allegations involving EchoStar), and implementing efficiency actions tied to a $65 million annualized run-rate cost reduction. Management also kept its post-close capital framework intact—planning about $1 billion of share repurchases and about $7 billion of debt repayment—to support its stated leverage target and investment-grade profile, while describing newer growth-adjacent initiatives like edge compute as being in an early “trial phase.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brent crude tops $100 as Iran attacks three ships, U.S. crude stocks post surprise draw
data.description:
Crude oil futures jumped Wednesday as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed, with Iran's Revolutionary Guard saying it fired on three vessels and seized two in the strait, and the latest weekly data showed a surprise draw in U.S. crude stockpiles.

Iran's parliament speaker and top negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf said a reopening of the strait was impossible while the U.S. continues its naval blockade of the country's ports, which he called "a flagrant ​breach of the ceasefire."

Reuters reported the U.S. military intercepted at least three Iranian-flagged tankers in ‌Asian waters and is redirecting them away from their positions near India, Malaysia and Sri Lanka.

Analysts said President Trump's extension of the ceasefire to give Iran more time to present a proposal for peace talks is seen as extending the time it takes for the strait to reopen.

"The lack of a peace deal or any plan moving forward has left the Strait of Hormuz and all energy assets in the Persian Gulf in a kind of limbo," Mizuho analyst Robert Yawger said in a note.

Trump's extension removes the immediate prospect of escalation, but delays talks "and likely keeps the U.S. blockade intact indefinitely," Ritterbusch analysts wrote, adding that "price-related demand destruction is becoming an increased topic of conversation."

The U.S. Energy Information Administration reported crude oil inventories rose last week as imports picked up from the week before and exports declined.

Commercial crude oil stocks excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve rose by 1.9M barrels to 465.7M barrels in the week ended April 17 and 3% above the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA said; crude stocks were expected to have fallen by ~1M barrels.

Gasoline inventories fell by a larger-than-expected 4.6M barrels at 228.4M and were 0.5% below the five-year average, the EIA said, and distillate fuel stocks also fell more than expected, down by 3.4M barrels to 108.1M barrels and 8% below the five-year average for the time of year.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for June delivery jumped 3.7% to $92.96/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery climbed 3.5% to $101.91/bbl, with both benchmarks rising for the third straight day.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) gained for the sixth consecutive session, although gains are small in the low-demand spring season; the front-month Nymex May contract rose 0.9% to $2.722/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)
